macro_segments.title: Just Measures and Weights: The Basic Mishna and the Problem of Deviating from Local Custom

micro_segments.title: Overview of the Mishna on proper measuring practices and the requirement to clean containers
Large versus small measures: why multiple small measurements benefit the buyer
The concept of lifnim mishurat hadin and consistency in measuring methods
Prohibition on smoothing in heaping places and heaping in smoothing places
The eifah shleima ruling from Scripture and why custom cannot be overridden with adjusted pricing
Even (weight measures) parallel to eifah (volume measures) with the same restrictions

macro_segments.title: The Midrashic Interpretation of Deuteronomy 25:14-15 and the Appointment of Market Officials

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda's midrash: two measures lead to poverty; just measures lead to wealth
Appointing agardomin (market inspectors) for measures but not for prices; free market principle
The house of the Nasi establishes officials for both measures and prices; Shmuel's correction via Karna
Rav Yitzchak's teaching that officials are needed for prices due to deception; the Rashbam's explanation
Historical examples of price gouging during religious observances and community responses

macro_segments.title: Proper Weighing Procedures: Weight Units, Combinations, and Scale Mechanics

micro_segments.title: Measuring in standard units (full, half, quarter pound) to avoid forced overbying or swapped measures
Three-quarter pound purchases: combining weights on the scale rather than three separate measurements
Ten-pound purchases: weighing all at once for a single favorable tip, not ten separate tips
The nefesh moznayim (scale mechanism): suspension height, distance from ground, and rope/bar proportions
Specifications for large-capacity scales (smiths), medium scales (wool/glass sellers), and small retail scales
Precious metal scales with unknown specifications but requiring greater precision and sensitivity

macro_segments.title: Construction Requirements for Measuring Equipment: Pans, Cleaning Tools, and Weight Composition

micro_segments.title: Prohibition on metal pans due to residue from moist goods; glass or ceramic alternatives
Exception: metal pans lined with leather are permitted for precious metals
The machak (cleaning tool): must be made of soft wood; prohibitions on gourds and metal
The machak must be uniform on both sides to ensure consistent cleaning practice
Proper cleaning technique: neither too fast (bad for seller) nor too slow (bad for buyer)
Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai's dilemma: publicizing cheating methods teaches wrongdoing but silence enables it

macro_segments.title: Deriving the Prohibition from Scripture: Land Measurement, Weight Storage, and Liquid Measures

micro_segments.title: Land measurement must be consistent: not measuring in summer for one and winter for another (rope stretching)
Weights must not be stored in salt, which either makes them heavier (Rashi) or lighter (Rabbeinu Tam)
Liquid measures must not be poured with froth; the transgression begins at the moment of improper pouring
Tosafot's question: why derive these from Scripture when they fall under the prohibition of theft?
Kal vachomer: if Torah is strict about the tiny mesura measure, how much more about larger measures

topical_tags.term: lifnim mishurat hadin (beyond the letter of the law)
free market economy and price regulation
